author: @thesecretworkshoppropstore7914
text: I use ABS a lot with my 3D printing. I also use a a fair amount of liquid ABS for helping with bed adhesion, as an ABS glue, and sometimes to make thin sheets of plastic.  All I do is soak ABS in acetone which dissolves it. If I then poor that into a tray and let it evaporate I can make plastic sheets that way.

author: @mcvirtual2574
text: Cold mold process those chips by adding acetone to make abs putty, used as a glue to make reinforced seems, or make actual pour-able paste and put in a mold that is acetone resistant (example -wax 2 part mold or hdpe) it takes ages to evaporate the acetone if you make it too thick so give it a couple of weeks, i have made blocks by putting inside a milk tetrapak carton, you can freeze it, leave it outside, work it with wood tools, fix cell cases, very useful, my latest project was a knob for a machine, all cold process.

author: @mikegLXIVMM
text:
I have dissolved ABS in acetone and placed the liquid it on a sheet of glass to make my own plastic film. 
If about 10% of that acetone has some xylene or similar, it will dry with more shine and a lot less bubbles.
Why not just use just xylene?
Takes longer to dry.

replies.author: @technosaurus3805

replies.author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
replies.text: @technosaurus3805 Yea, most people don't realize how energy intensive making cement is, from using old tires and plastics they could serve the world in reducing some ugly waste and hazardous fly ash with a fire hot enough to reduce ambient emissions.

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text:
@JamesWilson-pq9qp they have cement mixes now that are all fly ash and clinker - no Portland at all, but I think it is only used outdoors due to the possibility of off-gassing pollutants.  Still, that's a lot of roads, sidewalks and parking lots.

I actually ran a profitable recycling operation on a navy base and my last operations cost cutting measure was to reduce waste disposal cost.  We only got into early discussions with the city before I got my DD-214, but in my research I found it was more cost effective to burn our paper and plastic than to recycle it.

replies.author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
replies.text:
@technosaurus3805 So true, burning is the best option, as we saw in Texas and have seen in many other places over the years, garbage sitting is a fire hazard. I remember hearing if a fire is hot enough the emission will be broken down into base elements, even highly toxic compounds will change to simpler elements. Of course that's no paper fire. Tires are extremely difficult to extinguish.  ...  We still need to make good cheap Portland - can't depend on volcanoes and to dig up natural deposits. ....
Did your system boil water to turn a turbine? Low temperature fires will give us harmful emissions.

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
Yep, ABS filament is definitely used in 3D printing!!  I haven't used it yet, and likely won't for printing, as PETG is a better option for my needs.  Advantages of ABS are durability, impact resistance, heat resistance, easy to sand and glue, cheap price and ability to chemically "smooth" it with acetone vapors.  Disadvantages are that it warps/shrinks easily when printing, cracks easily if cooled too quickly, and smells a bit when heated.  There's a paradox in printing ABS in that you want good ventilation for the smell but you don't want windy conditions that might warp or crack the plastic as it prints.  For 3D printing, PETG has almost all the advantages of ABS with few of the disadvantages and is slightly more environmentally friendly.  
However, recycling ABS in the manner you're doing is fantastic!!  It will be a little bit more brittle when reheated like this, but more than serviceable to reheat and remold.  And MUCH better than sending it to a landfill where it will sit for 1000 years without breaking down.  Thanks for this video - I think it will be great if more people melt this down and reuse it than send it off to a landfill!!!  I ❤️❤️❤️ your recycling and re-purposing videos, I probably sound like a broken record, but I honestly think consumption is our biggest problem when it comes to both energy and pollution, and every bit of recycling makes our way of life more sustainable!!

author: @NwoDispatcher
text: What's about its melting point?

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Technically, it doesn't have a true melting point, as it's a "glassy solid".  But it begins to lose brittleness at around 100°C, can maintain a useful shape up to around 170°C, and starts extruding well around 220°C.  These temperatures can vary based on the brand.

author: @ezequieladrianminniti6252
text: Nice one. im working on a plastic beam making device. It consists of a squared metal tube wrapped in Kanthal heating coils properly insulated which heats up the tube up to 200C. Then you put plastic flakes into the tube push them with a stick inside until it's full the you heat up and press with manual or hydraulic press. Let cool down and you have a plastic beam!

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Nice!!

author: @tenij000
text: there are no waring or dangers stickers on abs spools for 3d printers

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: It's pretty safe to work with at printing temperatures, though it's definitely an irritant.  You really have to have some pretty high temperatures (like burning it) before it begins to produce toxic fumes/chemicals.  Anything below 300°C should be safe enough, but can cause respiratory irritation, headaches, nausea, etc.  And it's not pleasant smelling stuff.  Many people who print with it regularly build an enclosure for it, both to protect their prints from breezes (ABS is prone to warping/cracking if cooled quickly or unevenly) and to mitigate the fumes.
